ch1.dialog_1: Hey! So you want to start a lemonade stand?
ch1.dialog_2: Great! To start a business, you first need to understand the basics of accounting.
ch1.dialog_3: Let me teach you the most important equation: Assets = Liabilities + Equity
ch1.dialog_4: Simply put, 'What you own' = 'Whose money it is'.
ch1.dialog_5: Now, you need some starting capital. How much of your own money will you invest?
ch1.invest_500: You invested 500G! Cash (Asset) increased by 500, and Owner's Capital (Equity) also increased by 500.
ch1.invest_1000: Generous! 1,000G invested! Cash (Asset) and Owner's Capital (Equity) each increased by 1,000.
ch1.show_first_bs.msg: Let's look at the Balance Sheet! Check that Assets = Liabilities + Equity.
ch1.dialog_bs_explain: This equation always balances. That's why it's called a 'Balance Sheet'!
ch1.dialog_shop_1: Welcome! Looking for lemonade stand supplies?
ch1.dialog_shop_2: When you buy supplies, Cash decreases and Inventory increases. The composition of assets changes, but the total stays the same.
ch1.after_buy: See? The asset composition changed in the BS, but the total remains the same. That's the power of double-entry bookkeeping!
ch1.show_bs_after_buy.msg: Asset breakdown changed, but Total Assets = Liabilities + Equity stays the same!
ch1.dialog_sale_2: Your first customer! When you sell, Cash increases. But you also need to record the cost of materials used (Cost of Goods Sold).
ch1.choice_sale_price.prompt: Set your lemonade price (cost is 50G per cup)
ch1.after_sale: Your first sale! Notice that two journal entries occurred.
ch1.dialog_sale_explain_1: First: Cash increased and Revenue was recorded. Second: Inventory decreased and Cost of Goods Sold was recorded.
ch1.dialog_sale_explain_2: Revenue - COGS = Gross Profit. This is the basic measure of earnings!
ch1.more_sales: ...After that, several more customers came, and you sold 3 more cups of lemonade.
ch1.dialog_end_1: Day 1 is over! Let's check today's results on the Balance Sheet.
ch1.show_final_bs.msg: Final Balance Sheet. Does Assets = Liabilities + Equity hold?
ch1.dialog_bs_pl_link_1: Notice how 'Net Income' appears in the equity section of the BS? That's the profit calculated on the P&L. The profit you earn belongs to the owner, so it increases equity.
ch1.dialog_bs_pl_link_2: This means the BS and P&L are connected. 'Revenue - Expenses = Net Income' from the P&L flows into equity on the BS. That's the beauty of double-entry bookkeeping!
ch1.dialog_end_2: Let's summarize what you learned today:
ch1.dialog_summary_2: 2. Every transaction is recorded with double-entry (Debit = Credit)
ch1.dialog_summary_3: 3. Revenue - Cost of Goods Sold = Gross Profit
ch1.dialog_summary_4: 4. Net Income from the P&L flows into equity on the BS (the BS and P&L are connected)
ch1.dialog_end_final: Well done! In the next chapter, we'll learn about Profit and Cash Flow in detail!
ch1.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 1 Summary:

- The Accounting Equation: Assets = Liabilities + Equity
- Double-Entry: Every transaction has two sides
- How to read a Balance Sheet
- Revenue and Cost of Goods Sold
ch2.dialog_1: Good morning! Day 2. Today let's learn more about 'Profit'.
ch2.dialog_2: In business, what matters is 'how much profit you made'. The Income Statement calculates this.
ch2.dialog_3: Profit = Revenue - Expenses. Simple, but there are many types of expenses.
ch2.purchase_inventory_narration: First, let's stock up on supplies for today's sales. (8 cups worth, 50G each)
ch2.dialog_morning_sales: In the morning, you sold 5 cups of lemonade. (100G each, 50G cost)
ch2.dialog_gross_profit: Revenue 500G - COGS 250G = Gross Profit 250G. But this isn't the final profit.
ch2.dialog_expenses_intro: Running a business has other costs too. Like rent for your spot, and supplies like signs and cups.
ch2.choice_rent.prompt: Pay rent for your stand location
ch2.after_rent: Rent is recorded as an 'Expense'. When expenses increase, profit decreases. This is reflected in the Income Statement.
ch2.afternoon_narration: You continued selling in the afternoon. 3 more cups sold.
ch2.dialog_eod_1: Day 2 complete! Let's calculate today's profit.
ch2.dialog_eod_3: Gross Profit 400G - Rent = Net Income. This is the real 'profit'!
ch2.dialog_eod_4: Key point: Even when there's profit, cash and profit don't necessarily match.
ch2.dialog_eod_5: You still have inventory left, right? That's an asset, not an expense. Only the sold portion becomes COGS.
ch2.show_final_report.msg: Day 2 BS. Profit added to equity, and the equation still holds!
ch2.dialog_final: Excellent! In the next chapter, we'll learn about borrowing and credit transactions!
ch2.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 2 Summary:

- Income Statement
- Gross Profit = Revenue - COGS
- Operating Expenses (Rent, etc.)
- Net Income = Gross Profit - Expenses
- Profit != Cash
ch3.dialog_1: Day 3. I have something important to discuss. To serve more customers, you need more supplies.
ch3.dialog_2: But you might not have enough cash. In that case, there's an option: borrowing.
ch3.dialog_3: When you borrow from a bank, Cash (Asset) increases, but Loans Payable (Liability) also increases.
ch3.banker_dialog_500: A 500G loan. Interest rate is 10%, so interest will be 50G. Approved.
ch3.banker_dialog_1000: A 1,000G loan. Interest rate is 10%, so interest will be 100G. Approved.
ch3.after_loan: Check the BS. Cash (Asset) increased, but Loans Payable (Liability) increased by the same amount. The equation holds!
ch3.show_bs_after_loan.msg: BS after borrowing: Assets increased, but so did Liabilities!
ch3.supplier_dialog_1: If you're buying a lot, I can offer credit! Just pay me later.
ch3.dialog_credit_explain: A 'Credit Purchase' means receiving supplies without paying cash. Inventory (Asset) increases and Accounts Payable (Liability) increases.
ch3.after_credit_purchase: Notice! You didn't use any cash, yet both assets and liabilities increased. The equation always balances!
ch3.sales_narration: With plenty of supplies, you sold 10 cups today! (100G each, 50G cost)
ch3.pay_ap: Now that you have cash from sales, let's pay off the supplier.
ch3.dialog_ap_explain: Paying AP reduces both Cash (Asset) and AP (Liability). The equation stays balanced!
ch3.loan_repay_intro: Let's also repay the bank. You need to pay principal plus interest. Interest is an 'Expense'.
ch3.dialog_interest_explain: Interest is an expense that reduces profit. But the {borrowAmount}G principal is just repaying a liability, not an expense. This distinction is important!
ch3.show_final_bs.msg: Day 3 BS. Liabilities back to 0, and equity grew from profit!
ch3.dialog_summary: Let's organize today's liability types: Loans Payable (from bank) and Accounts Payable (from supplier). Both are 'Liabilities' on the right side of the BS.
ch3.dialog_final: Well done! You've mastered the accounting basics. In the next chapter, we'll learn more advanced transactions!
ch3.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 3 Summary:

- Bank Loan: Cash(+) / Loans Payable(+)
- Credit Purchase: Inventory(+) / AP(+)
- Pay AP: AP(-) / Cash(-)
- Loan Repayment: Loans(-) + Interest Exp / Cash(-)
- Interest is an expense, principal repayment is not
ch4.dialog_1: Today let's learn about 'Accrual Accounting'. The timing of receiving money and recognizing revenue are different.
ch4.dialog_2: Under cash basis, revenue is recorded when cash is received. But under accrual basis, revenue is recognized when goods are delivered.
ch4.dialog_3: This means 'credit sales' become possible. You deliver goods with a promise that the customer pays later.
ch4.dialog_customer_1: I want to buy a lot of lemonade, but I don't have cash right now. Can I pay later?
ch4.dialog_mentor_credit: This is a credit sale! Accounts Receivable (an asset) increases, and revenue is recorded at the same time.
ch4.purchase_inventory_narration: Let's stock up on supplies before the credit sales.
ch4.choice_credit_sale.prompt: Decide the credit sale amount (cost is half)
ch4.after_credit_sale: Notice! Cash didn't increase at all, yet revenue was recorded. This is accrual accounting.
ch4.show_bs_after_credit_sale.msg: Check BS: A/R (asset) increased, equity grew from profit. Cash unchanged!
ch4.collection_narration: ...A few days later, the customer comes with partial payment.
ch4.dialog_after_collection: A/R decreased and Cash increased. Just an asset swap - total is the same. No new revenue recorded!
ch4.additional_credit_narration: Another customer comes to buy lemonade on credit.
ch4.bad_debt_intro: By the way... credit sales carry risk. Sometimes customers cannot pay.
ch4.bad_debt_narration: The other customer contacted you saying "I cannot pay"...
ch4.bad_debt_dialog: This is a 'bad debt'. Uncollectible A/R is written off as 'Bad Debt Expense'.
ch4.after_bad_debt: A/R (asset) decreased, Bad Debt Expense increased. More expense means less profit. This is the risk of credit sales.
ch4.dialog_summary_1: Today's summary. Three key points of accrual accounting:
ch4.dialog_summary_2: 1. Revenue is recognized when goods are delivered (not when cash is received)
ch4.dialog_summary_3: 2. Collecting A/R is not revenue - it is an asset swap
ch4.dialog_summary_4: 3. Bad debts are expenses that directly reduce profit
ch4.show_final_bs.msg: Final BS: If A/R remains, there is still money to collect!
ch4.dialog_final: Well done! Next chapter, we'll learn about service businesses and unearned revenue!
ch4.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 4 Summary:

- Accrual Accounting: Revenue recognized at delivery
- Credit Sale: A/R(+) / Revenue(+)
- A/R Collection: Cash(+) / A/R(-)
- Bad Debt: Bad Debt Exp(+) / A/R(-)
- Credit sales carry risk
ch5.dialog_1: Today we try something new! Let's start a delivery service alongside lemonade.
ch5.dialog_2: Service businesses sell 'services', not goods. No inventory is needed.
ch5.dialog_3: Service Revenue is a separate revenue account from Sales Revenue.
ch5.dialog_customer_1: I would like to use your delivery service!
ch5.after_service_sale: Notice! No COGS. Services don't have inventory. Just cash and revenue directly.
ch5.hire_intro: Doing deliveries alone is tough. Let's hire an employee. Their wages are an expense.
ch5.after_wages: Wages are an expense. Labor is the main cost in service businesses.
ch5.dialog_unearned_1: I want to prepay for all next week's deliveries! How about 400G?
ch5.dialog_unearned_2: This is important! You receive cash before providing the service. This is called 'Unearned Revenue'.
ch5.dialog_unearned_3: Unearned Revenue is a 'Liability'. Because you still have an obligation to provide the service.
ch5.show_bs_unearned.msg: BS: Cash (asset) increased, but Unearned Revenue (liability) also increased. This is not revenue!
ch5.dialog_earn_intro: ...A few days later, you provided half of the delivery services.
ch5.dialog_earn_explain: For the portion of service delivered, we reduce Unearned Revenue (liability) and transfer it to Service Revenue.
ch5.after_earn: Unearned Revenue (liability) decreased by 200, Service Revenue increased by 200. The remaining 200 is still a liability!
ch5.dialog_summary_1: Remember revenue recognition timing: Revenue is recognized when service is 'delivered'!
ch5.dialog_summary_2: Even if you receive advance payment, it's a 'liability' until the service is provided.
ch5.show_final_bs.msg: Final BS: Check that Unearned Revenue (liability) still remains!
ch5.dialog_final: Next chapter, we'll learn about inventory valuation methods (FIFO and LIFO)!
ch5.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 5 Summary:

- Service Business: No inventory
- Service Revenue: Cash(+) / Service Rev(+)
- Wages are expenses: Wages Exp(+) / Cash(-)
- Unearned Revenue: Cash(+) / Unearned Rev(liability)(+)
- Revenue Recognition: Transfer from unearned when service delivered
ch6.dialog_1: Today we learn inventory valuation. The same supplies can have different costs depending on when they were purchased.
ch6.dialog_2: The question is: 'Which purchase cost do we use as COGS when we sell?'
ch6.dialog_supplier_1: Lemon supplies are still 50G per cup as usual.
ch6.price_increase_narration: ...The following week, you hear from the supplier.
ch6.dialog_price_increase: Sorry, but prices went up! It will be 80G per cup now.
ch6.dialog_inventory_status: Now inventory has two cost layers: 5 cups @50G and 5 cups @80G. Total 650G inventory.
ch6.dialog_sell_intro: A customer wants to buy 3 cups of lemonade! Selling price is 150G each.
ch6.dialog_fifo_lifo: Now, what is the COGS? This is where the valuation method matters!
ch6.fifo_explain: FIFO! First purchased items are sold first. All 3 cups come from @50G inventory. COGS = 3 x 50 = 150G.
ch6.fifo_result: FIFO: Revenue 450 - COGS 150 = Gross Profit 300G! Remaining inventory: 500G (2@50 + 5@80).
ch6.lifo_explain: LIFO! Last purchased items are sold first. All 3 cups come from @80G inventory. COGS = 3 x 80 = 240G.
ch6.lifo_result: LIFO: Revenue 450 - COGS 240 = Gross Profit 210G! Remaining inventory: 410G (5@50 + 2@80).
ch6.dialog_compare_1: Let's compare! FIFO gross profit is 300G, LIFO is 210G. Same sales, different profit depending on method!
ch6.dialog_compare_2: When prices rise: FIFO shows higher profit, LIFO shows lower profit. Inventory values are reversed.
ch6.dialog_compare_3: In Japan, FIFO and moving average are mainly used. In the US, LIFO is also allowed.
ch6.show_final_bs.msg: BS: Inventory method affects both asset values and profit!
ch6.dialog_final: Next chapter, we'll learn about fixed assets and depreciation!
ch6.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 6 Summary:

- Same goods can have different purchase costs
- FIFO: Older costs expensed first (higher profit)
- LIFO: Newer costs expensed first (lower profit)
- Inventory method affects both profit and BS
- FIFO is standard in Japan
ch7.dialog_1: Business is growing! Let's invest in equipment to produce more efficiently.
ch7.dialog_2: Equipment is recorded as an 'Asset', not an 'Expense'. Because it can be used for years.
ch7.dialog_3: This is the difference between 'Capital Expenditure' and 'Revenue Expenditure'. Equipment is capital, repairs are expense.
ch7.dialog_supplier: Looking for equipment? We have a mixer and a deluxe stand!
ch7.after_purchase: Equipment now appears on BS as an asset! Cash decreased, Equipment increased. Just an asset swap.
ch7.show_bs_after_purchase.msg: BS: Equipment recorded as an asset!
ch7.dialog_dep_1: But equipment loses value over time. This is called 'Depreciation'.
ch7.dialog_dep_2: For example, if equipment is used for 5 periods, 1/5 of the cost becomes expense each period. This is straight-line depreciation.
ch7.dialog_dep_3: Depreciation Expense increases, and Accumulated Depreciation (contra-asset) increases. The book value of equipment decreases.
ch7.dep_period_1_narration: ...The first period ends, and depreciation is recorded.
ch7.dep_period_1_narration_500: ...The first period ends, and depreciation is recorded. (Deluxe Stand 500G ÷ 5 years = 100G/period)
ch7.dialog_after_dep_1: Accumulated Depreciation is a 'contra-asset'. Subtract it from equipment cost to get book value (net value).
ch7.show_bs_after_dep_1.msg: BS: Accumulated Depreciation reducing equipment value!
ch7.dep_period_2_narration: ...The second period also ends. Recording depreciation again.
ch7.dep_period_2_narration_500: ...The second period also ends. Recording depreciation again. (Deluxe Stand 500G ÷ 5 years = 100G/period)
ch7.dialog_after_dep_2: Two periods of depreciation totaling 120G. Equipment's book value is cost - 120G.
ch7.dialog_capital_vs_expense: Let's clarify an important distinction: 'buying' equipment vs 'paying' rent.
ch7.dialog_capital_1: Equipment purchase: Becomes an asset, gradually expensed (depreciation)
ch7.dialog_capital_2: Rent payment: Immediately becomes full expense (current period expense)
ch7.dialog_capital_3: Long-lasting items are assets (capital expenditure), immediately consumed items are expenses (revenue expenditure).
ch7.show_final_bs.msg: Final BS: Check the relationship between Equipment (cost) and Accumulated Depreciation!
ch7.dialog_final: Next chapter, we'll understand the difference between profits and cash more deeply!
ch7.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 7 Summary:

- Equipment is an asset (not expense)
- Depreciation: Allocating equipment cost over periods
- Dep.Exp(+) / Accum.Dep(+)
- Book Value = Cost - Accumulated Depreciation
- Capital Expenditure vs Revenue Expenditure
ch8.dialog_1: Today's topic is crucial: 'Why can you have profit but still run low on cash?'
ch8.dialog_2: Profit and cash are different things. Under accrual accounting, cash movements and profit recognition timing don't match.
ch8.dialog_3: Let's look at an example: prepaying next month's rent this month.
ch8.dialog_prepaid_1: Let's prepay next month's rent of 200G. This is recorded as 'Prepaid Expenses' (asset).
ch8.dialog_prepaid_2: Cash decreased by 200G, but no expense yet! Only Prepaid Expenses (asset) increased.
ch8.show_bs_prepaid.msg: BS: Prepaid is an asset. Cash decreased but no impact on profit!
ch8.expense_recognition: ...Next month arrives. The prepaid period has come.
ch8.dialog_expense_1: It's next month now, so we transfer Prepaid to Rent Expense. This time it affects profit!
ch8.dialog_expense_2: Notice! Cash didn't move this time. Prepaid (asset) decreased, Rent Expense increased. Cash was already paid earlier.
ch8.dialog_analysis_1: Now you understand the difference between profit and cash. Let's review.
ch8.dialog_analysis_2: Cases where you have profit but low cash: Credit sales (revenue but no cash), buying inventory (cash out but no expense), equipment purchase (cash out but capitalized).
ch8.dialog_analysis_3: Cases where cash increases but not profit: Borrowing (cash up but liability), advance payments (cash up but liability), A/R collection (cash up but not revenue).
ch8.dialog_cf_1: 1. Operating Activities: Cash from core business (sales, purchases, wages, etc.)
ch8.dialog_cf_2: 2. Investing Activities: Cash for long-term assets (buying/selling equipment)
ch8.dialog_cf_3: 3. Financing Activities: Cash from borrowing, repaying, or investor contributions
ch8.dialog_cf_4: A healthy business generates positive cash flow from operations. This is the most important!
ch8.show_final_bs.msg: BS: Cash balance and Retained Earnings (cumulative profit) do not necessarily match!
ch8.dialog_final: You're getting the big picture of accrual accounting. Next, let's learn about taxes!
ch8.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 8 Summary:

- Profit and Cash are different
- Prepaid: Pay cash now, expense later
- Credit sales/inventory/equipment: Profit but low cash
- Borrowing/advances: Cash up but not profit
- Operating/Investing/Financing cash flows
ch9.dialog_1: Today's unavoidable topic: 'Taxes'. When you make a profit, you need to pay taxes.
ch9.dialog_2: Pre-tax profit minus taxes equals 'After-tax profit'. This is the amount that ultimately remains in equity.
ch9.dialog_3: First, let's check the pre-tax profit, then decide the tax rate.
ch9.dialog_taxman_1: Tax office here. Taxes apply to your business profit. Let us determine the tax rate.
ch9.tax_20: 20% tax rate. If pre-tax profit is 500G, tax would be 100G.
ch9.tax_30: 30% tax rate. If pre-tax profit is 500G, tax would be 150G.
ch9.after_tax: Taxes are recorded as an expense. Pre-tax profit - Tax = After-tax profit (Net Income).
ch9.dialog_after_tax_2: Paying taxes is a civic duty. Make sure to pay when you earn profits.
ch9.show_bs_after_tax.msg: BS: After tax payment. Cash decreased, expense reduced equity.
ch9.dialog_liq_1: Now, let's think about what happens if you completely close (liquidate) the business.
ch9.dialog_liq_2: Liquidation means: 1) Convert all assets to cash 2) Pay all liabilities 3) Remaining cash belongs to the owner.
ch9.dialog_liq_3: In other words: Assets - Liabilities = Equity (owner's share). The accounting equation itself!
ch9.dialog_liq_4: For example, sell all inventory, sell equipment, collect all A/R, then pay all liabilities.
ch9.dialog_liq_5: The remaining amount is equity: the initial investment (Owner's Capital) + earned profits (Retained Earnings).
ch9.show_final_bs.msg: BS: Equity = Owner's Capital + Retained Earnings. This is the owner's true share!
ch9.dialog_final: In the final chapter, we'll do a comprehensive analysis with financial ratios and graduate!
ch9.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 9 Summary:

- Taxes are recorded as expenses
- Pre-tax Profit - Tax = After-tax Profit
- Liquidation: Sell assets -> Pay liabilities -> Remainder is owner's share
- Assets - Liabilities = Equity (Accounting Equation)
ch10.dialog_1: Congratulations! It's the final chapter. Let's review everything and learn financial analysis!
ch10.dialog_2: First, let's review what we've learned.
ch10.review_ch1: Ch.1: Accounting Equation - Assets = Liabilities + Equity. Double-entry basics.
ch10.review_ch2: Ch.2: Income - Revenue - Expenses = Profit. Profit differs from cash.
ch10.review_ch3: Ch.3: Liabilities - Loans and AP. Interest is expense, principal repayment is not.
ch10.review_ch4_5: Ch.4-5: Accrual - Credit sales, A/R, Unearned Revenue, Service Revenue. Timing matters.
ch10.review_ch6_7: Ch.6-7: Asset Valuation - Inventory methods (FIFO/LIFO), Fixed assets and depreciation.
ch10.review_ch8_9: Ch.8-9: Cash vs Profit, Taxes, Liquidation. The full picture of accrual accounting.
ch10.show_review_bs.msg: BS across all chapters. Assets = Liabilities + Equity always holds!
ch10.ratio_intro: Finally, let's learn 'Financial Ratios'. Using numbers to analyze business health.
ch10.dialog_ratio_1: Profit Margin: Net Income / Revenue x 100. What % of revenue became profit.
ch10.dialog_ratio_2: Current Ratio: Current Assets / Current Liabilities. Shows short-term payment ability. Above 1 is desirable.
ch10.dialog_ratio_3: Debt Ratio: Total Liabilities / Total Assets. Dependency on debt. Lower is safer.
ch10.dialog_ratio_4: Return on Assets (ROA): Net Income / Total Assets x 100. How much you earned with your assets.
ch10.quiz_intro: Now for a final quiz! Let's check your understanding.
ch10.quiz_1.prompt: How can you improve Profit Margin?
ch10.quiz_1_correct: Correct! Margin = Profit / Revenue, so increase revenue or cut costs to improve!
ch10.quiz_1_wrong: Not quite! Borrowing increases liabilities but doesn't directly affect profit margin. The answer is 'increase revenue or cut costs'.
ch10.quiz_2_correct: Correct! Depreciation allocates the acquisition cost of equipment over its useful life!
ch10.quiz_2_wrong: Close! Repairs are immediately expensed. Depreciation 'allocates acquisition cost over periods'.
ch10.quiz_3.prompt: Where does Unearned Revenue appear on the BS?
ch10.quiz_3_correct: Correct! It's a 'Liability' because you still owe the service! It becomes revenue after delivery.
ch10.quiz_3_wrong: Not revenue yet! Until the service is delivered, it's a 'Liability'. It becomes revenue after delivery.
ch10.dialog_grad_1: You've completed all 10 chapters! The accounting journey that started from a lemonade stand reaches its milestone.
ch10.dialog_grad_2: Remember: Accounting is the 'language of business'. With these basics, you can understand any business's finances.
ch10.dialog_grad_3: Assets = Liabilities + Equity. Never forget this equation!
ch10.show_final_bs.msg: Final BS: Your lemonade stand's completed financial statement!
ch10.dialog_final: Congratulations, graduate! You've taken the first step toward accounting mastery. Keep it up!
ch10.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 10 - Final Analysis:

- Review of all 9 chapters
- Profit Margin: Net Income / Revenue
- Current Ratio: Current Assets / Current Liabilities
- Debt Ratio: Liabilities / Assets
- ROA: Net Income / Total Assets

Game Complete! Congratulations!
ch1.show_first_pl.msg: Your first Income Statement (P&L)! Check revenue and COGS.
ch1.dialog_pl_explain: While the BS shows 'financial position at a point in time', the P&L shows 'business performance over a period'. Revenue - Expenses = Net Income!
ch2.explain_revenue_account: 'Sales Revenue' is a revenue account. It records income from selling goods. It appears at the top of the P&L.
ch2.explain_cogs_account: 'Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)' is an expense account. It records the purchase cost of goods sold. Revenue - COGS = Gross Profit.
ch2.show_mid_pl.msg: Morning P&L: Sales Revenue and COGS reflected!
ch2.explain_rent_account: 'Rent Expense' is an operating expense account. It's deducted after Gross Profit, separate from COGS.
ch2.show_pl_after_rent.msg: P&L after rent: Expenses increased, Net Income decreased!
ch2.show_final_pl.msg: Day 2 final P&L. Revenue - COGS - Rent = Net Income. Today's business performance!
ch3.explain_loans_payable: 'Loans Payable' is a liability on the BS. Money borrowed from the bank with an obligation to repay. Borrowing itself doesn't affect the P&L, but interest does.
ch3.explain_accounts_payable: 'Accounts Payable' is also a liability. It represents credit from suppliers that must be paid within a set period.
ch3.show_final_pl.msg: Day 3 P&L: Interest expense reduces Net Income!
ch4.explain_accounts_receivable: 'Accounts Receivable' is an asset. It represents the right to collect payment for goods sold on credit. Cash hasn't been received, but revenue is recognized.
ch4.explain_bad_debt_account: 'Bad Debt Expense' is an expense account. It records uncollectible receivables as a cost. This is the risk cost of credit sales.
ch4.show_pl_after_bad_debt.msg: P&L after bad debt: Expenses increased, Net Income decreased!
ch5.explain_service_revenue: 'Service Revenue' is a separate revenue account from Sales Revenue. It records income from providing services rather than selling goods. No COGS is involved.
ch5.explain_wages_account: 'Wages Expense' is an expense account. It records labor costs paid to employees and is often the largest cost in service businesses.
ch5.show_mid_pl.msg: Service Revenue and Wages reflected in the P&L!
ch5.show_final_pl.msg: Final P&L: Service Revenue + Sales - All Expenses = Net Income!
ch6.explain_inventory_deep: Inventory is a BS asset account. It holds unsold goods at cost. Only when sold does it become COGS on the P&L. The valuation method (FIFO/LIFO) changes the amount!
ch6.show_bs_mid.msg: BS: Inventory with two different cost layers!
ch6.show_pl_compare.msg: P&L: Inventory method changes COGS, which changes Gross Profit!
ch6.show_final_pl.msg: Final P&L: Valuation method difference impacts Net Income!
ch7.explain_equipment_account: 'Equipment' is a BS asset account. A tangible fixed asset used long-term. The full cost is an asset at purchase, gradually expensed through depreciation.
ch7.explain_depreciation_accounts: 'Depreciation Expense' is a P&L expense, 'Accumulated Depreciation' is a contra-asset on BS. Together they represent the periodic allocation of equipment cost.
ch8.explain_prepaid_account: 'Prepaid Expenses' is a BS asset account. Cash paid in advance for services not yet received. It's an asset at payment, then transfers to expense when the period arrives.
ch8.show_pl_after_expense.msg: P&L: Prepaid converted to Rent Expense! No cash movement but P&L is affected.
ch8.dialog_cash_vs_pl: Key insight: The timing of cash payment and P&L expense recognition differ. This is the essence of accrual accounting!
ch8.show_final_pl.msg: Final P&L: Expenses properly allocated across periods under accrual basis!
ch9.explain_tax_expense: 'Tax Expense' is a P&L expense account. Calculated by applying the tax rate to pre-tax profit. Different from COGS or operating expenses, but still reduces Net Income.
ch9.show_pl_after_tax.msg: P&L after tax: Pre-tax Profit - Tax = After-tax Profit (Net Income)!
ch9.dialog_pl_tax_explain: The Net Income at the bottom of the P&L flows into Retained Earnings on the BS. P&L and BS are connected through Net Income!
ch9.show_final_pl.msg: Final P&L: After-tax Net Income flows to BS equity!
ch9.dialog_pl_to_equity: P&L Net Income is added to BS Retained Earnings. P&L measures flow over a period, BS measures stock at a point in time.
ch10.show_review_pl.msg: P&L across all chapters. All revenues and expenses at a glance!
ch10.dialog_pl_bs_connection: P&L Net Income becomes BS Retained Earnings. P&L and BS are closely linked. This is the complete picture of financial statements!
ch10.show_final_pl.msg: Final P&L: Your lemonade stand's completed Income Statement!
ch201.initial_fund_narration: The Lemonade Group received additional capital from shareholders to fund factory operations for the expansion.
ch201.initial_fund_done: We received 50,000 in additional capital! Checking Account (asset) increased and Owner's Capital (equity) increased. We now have sufficient funds for factory operations.
ch201.dialog_1: Welcome to the factory! This is where the real magic of manufacturing happens.
ch201.dialog_2: In a manufacturing business, we need to track the cost of making products, not just buying and selling them.
ch201.dialog_3: Have you ever wondered how companies figure out how much it costs to make a single product?
ch201.dialog_4: That is what cost accounting is all about. Let me show you the basics using our lemonade factory.
ch201.dialog_cost_elements: Manufacturing costs have three main elements. Let me explain each one.
ch201.dialog_materials: First: Direct Materials. These are the raw ingredients that go directly into the product, like lemons and sugar.
ch201.dialog_labor: Second: Direct Labor. These are the wages paid to workers who directly produce the goods, like the factory workers.
ch201.dialog_overhead: Third: Manufacturing Overhead. These are indirect costs like factory rent, utilities, and equipment depreciation.
ch201.purchase_materials_intro: Let us start by purchasing raw materials for our lemonade production. We will buy 3,000G worth of lemons and sugar.
ch201.dialog_after_purchase: Raw Materials is an asset account. The materials sit in storage until we use them in production.
ch201.show_bs_after_purchase.msg: BS after material purchase: Raw Materials (asset) increased, Cash decreased.
ch201.issue_materials_intro: Now we issue 2,000G of raw materials to the production floor. This moves costs from Raw Materials to Work in Process.
ch201.dialog_after_issue: Work in Process (WIP) represents products currently being manufactured. Materials moved from storage to the factory floor.
ch201.labor_intro: Workers on the production line earned 1,500G in wages. This direct labor cost goes straight into WIP.
ch201.dialog_after_labor: Direct labor is added to WIP because these wages are directly tied to producing our lemonade.
ch201.overhead_intro: The factory also incurs overhead costs: electricity, equipment maintenance, and other indirect expenses totaling 800G.
ch201.dialog_after_overhead: Manufacturing Overhead collects all indirect production costs. These cannot be directly traced to a specific product.
ch201.complete_production_intro: Production is complete! The lemonade is bottled and ready. Let us transfer all costs to Finished Goods.
ch201.dialog_after_complete: Finished Goods now holds the total manufacturing cost: Materials 2,000 + Labor 1,500 + Overhead 800 = 4,300G.
ch201.show_bs_after_production.msg: BS after production: Raw Materials and WIP cleared, Finished Goods holds the total cost!
ch201.quiz_intro: Time for a quick quiz to check your understanding of manufacturing costs!
ch201.quiz_cost_elements.question: Which of the following correctly lists the three elements of manufacturing cost?
ch201.quiz_cost_elements.0: Sales cost, administrative cost, financial cost
ch201.quiz_cost_elements.1: Direct materials, direct labor, manufacturing overhead
ch201.quiz_cost_elements.2: Raw materials, finished goods, work in process
ch201.quiz_cost_elements.correct: Correct! The three cost elements are direct materials, direct labor, and manufacturing overhead.
ch201.quiz_cost_elements.incorrect: Not quite. The three manufacturing cost elements are: direct materials, direct labor, and manufacturing overhead.
ch201.dialog_summary_1: Let us review the flow: Raw Materials -> Work in Process -> Finished Goods. Costs accumulate as products move through production.
ch201.dialog_summary_2: The three cost elements (materials, labor, overhead) are all collected in WIP, then transferred to Finished Goods when production is done.
ch201.dialog_final: Great work! You now understand the basics of manufacturing cost accounting. Next, we will dive deeper into cost accounting methods!
ch201.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 201 Summary:

- Three cost elements: Direct Materials, Direct Labor, Manufacturing Overhead
- Cost flow: Raw Materials -> WIP -> Finished Goods
- Direct costs trace to products; overhead is indirect
- Total product cost = Materials + Labor + Overhead
ch202.dialog_1: Now that you understand cost elements, let us explore how to assign those costs to products.
ch202.dialog_2: Different manufacturing processes require different costing methods. Today we will learn two major approaches.
ch202.dialog_3: The two main methods are Job Order Costing and Process Costing. Each fits a different type of production.
ch202.dialog_job_order: Job Order Costing tracks costs for each individual job or batch. Think custom furniture or special orders. Each job gets its own cost sheet.
ch202.dialog_process_costing: Process Costing averages costs across mass-produced identical items. Think our lemonade bottles: every unit is the same, so we spread costs evenly.
ch202.dialog_overhead_alloc_intro: One tricky part of cost accounting is allocating overhead. Overhead costs are shared across products.
ch202.dialog_overhead_alloc_explain: We allocate overhead using a predetermined rate. For example, if overhead is based on labor hours, we assign overhead proportionally to each product.
ch202.dialog_after_alloc: Overhead is now assigned to WIP. The overhead account is credited (reduced) and WIP is debited (increased).
ch202.journal_entry_overhead.prompt: Record the journal entry for allocating 1,000G of manufacturing overhead to Work in Process.
ch202.journal_entry_overhead.correct: Perfect! Debit WIP and credit Manufacturing Overhead to allocate indirect costs to production.
ch202.journal_entry_overhead.incorrect: Not quite. To allocate overhead: Debit Work in Process (cost goes in) and Credit Manufacturing Overhead (cost comes out).
ch202.journal_entry_overhead.hint: Overhead allocation moves costs from the overhead account into WIP. Which account increases (debit) and which decreases (credit)?
ch202.transfer_intro: Production is complete. Let us transfer the total cost of 5,000G from WIP to Finished Goods.
ch202.dialog_after_transfer: All production costs now sit in Finished Goods, ready to become Cost of Goods Sold when we make a sale.
ch202.show_bs_after_transfer.msg: BS: WIP cleared out, Finished Goods holds the completed product cost!
ch202.sale_intro: A customer wants to buy our entire batch! Let us record the sale and the corresponding cost.
ch202.dialog_customer: I will take the whole batch of lemonade! Here is 8,000G.
ch202.dialog_after_sale: Two entries for a sale: Revenue is recorded, and Finished Goods becomes Cost of Goods Sold.
ch202.dialog_profit_explain: The gross profit of 3,000G represents the markup over manufacturing cost. This is the value added by our production process.
ch202.dialog_summary_1: Cost accounting tracks costs through the manufacturing process: from raw materials to WIP to finished goods to COGS.
ch202.dialog_summary_2: Job order costing is for unique products, process costing for identical mass-produced goods. Overhead must be allocated using a systematic method.
ch202.dialog_final: Excellent! Next we will learn about standard costing and how to analyze variances from expected costs!
ch202.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 202 Summary:

- Job Order Costing: Tracks costs per job/batch
- Process Costing: Averages costs across identical units
- Overhead Allocation: Assign indirect costs to products
- Cost flow: Materials + Labor + Overhead -> WIP -> Finished Goods -> COGS
ch203.dialog_1: Today we tackle an important management tool: Standard Costing.
ch203.dialog_2: Imagine you could predict exactly how much each product should cost to make. That is what a standard cost system does.
ch203.dialog_3: By comparing standard (expected) costs to actual costs, managers can identify problems and improve efficiency.
ch203.dialog_standard_explain: A standard cost is a predetermined cost for materials, labor, and overhead. It is based on expected prices and expected usage quantities.
ch203.dialog_standard_purpose: The purpose is performance evaluation. If actual costs exceed standards, something went wrong. If actual costs are lower, something went well.
ch203.standard_cost_intro: Let us set up standard costs for our lemonade. The standard cost per batch is 5,000G. We record production at this standard amount.
ch203.dialog_after_standard: WIP is recorded at the standard (expected) cost. Later, we will compare this to what we actually spent.
ch203.show_bs_after_standard.msg: BS: WIP recorded at standard cost of 5,000G.
ch203.variance_intro: Now let us check what actually happened. The actual costs came in differently from the standards!
ch203.dialog_actual_cost: The actual cost was 5,300G instead of 5,000G! We overspent by 300G. We need to analyze why.
ch203.dialog_price_variance: Material Price Variance: We paid more per unit of raw materials than expected. The price difference was 200G unfavorable.
ch203.dialog_efficiency_variance: Labor Efficiency Variance: Workers took longer than expected, adding 100G in extra labor cost. Also unfavorable.
ch203.variance_tx_intro: Let us record these variances. Each type of variance gets its own account for tracking.
ch203.dialog_after_variance: The variance accounts capture the difference between standard and actual costs. This helps managers pinpoint issues.
ch203.dialog_favorable_unfavorable: Variances can be favorable (actual < standard, saving money) or unfavorable (actual > standard, overspending). Both need investigation!
ch203.quiz_intro: Let us test your understanding of variance analysis!
ch203.quiz_variance.question: If actual material cost is lower than standard cost, what kind of variance is it?
ch203.quiz_variance.correct: Correct! When actual cost is less than standard, it is a favorable variance. You spent less than expected!
ch203.quiz_variance.incorrect: Not quite. When actual cost is LESS than standard, it means you saved money, which is a favorable variance.
ch203.dialog_summary_1: Standard costing sets predetermined costs for materials, labor, and overhead. Comparing to actual costs reveals variances.
ch203.dialog_summary_2: Favorable variances mean actual < standard (good). Unfavorable variances mean actual > standard (needs attention). Both require analysis.
ch203.dialog_final: Well done! Next, we will learn about break-even analysis and how to determine the point where your business turns profitable!
ch203.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 203 Summary:

- Standard Cost: Predetermined expected cost per unit
- Variance = Actual Cost - Standard Cost
- Favorable: Actual < Standard (saved money)
- Unfavorable: Actual > Standard (overspent)
- Price Variance: Difference in input prices
- Efficiency Variance: Difference in input quantities
ch204.dialog_1: Today we learn one of the most powerful tools in management accounting: Break-Even Analysis.
ch204.dialog_2: Every business needs to know: How much do we need to sell just to cover all our costs? That is the break-even point.
ch204.dialog_3: To find it, we first need to understand the difference between variable costs and fixed costs.
ch204.dialog_variable_cost: Variable costs change with production volume. More lemonade produced means more materials and labor needed. Examples: raw materials, direct labor, packaging.
ch204.dialog_fixed_cost: Fixed costs stay the same regardless of production volume. Even if you produce zero units, you still pay these. Examples: rent, insurance, depreciation.
ch204.dialog_example_intro: Let us record some of each type to see the difference. First, fixed costs that occur regardless of output.
ch204.dialog_after_fixed: Rent and depreciation are fixed costs: 2,500G total regardless of how many units we produce.
ch204.dialog_after_variable: Materials and direct labor are variable costs: 4,500G for this production volume. If volume doubles, these costs roughly double too.
ch204.show_pl_costs.msg: P&L: Fixed costs (2,500G) + Variable costs (4,500G) = Total costs 7,000G.
ch204.contribution_margin_intro: Now, let us learn about the Contribution Margin. This is key to break-even analysis.
ch204.dialog_contribution_calc: Contribution Margin = Sales Revenue - Variable Costs. If selling price is 100G per unit and variable cost is 45G, the contribution margin per unit is 55G.
ch204.dialog_contribution_ratio: Contribution Margin Ratio = Contribution Margin / Sales Revenue = 55/100 = 55%. This means 55 cents of every dollar earned goes toward covering fixed costs and profit.
ch204.breakeven_intro: With the contribution margin, we can calculate the break-even point.
ch204.dialog_breakeven_formula: Break-Even Sales = Fixed Costs / Contribution Margin Ratio. This tells us the revenue needed to cover all costs.
ch204.dialog_breakeven_example: With fixed costs of 2,500G and a 55% contribution margin ratio: Break-Even Sales = 2,500 / 0.55 = approximately 4,545G.
ch204.dialog_breakeven_meaning: That means we need to sell about 4,545G worth of lemonade just to break even. Every sale beyond that is pure profit!
ch204.choice_scenario.prompt: How should we improve profitability beyond break-even?
ch204.scenario_more_sales: Good choice! Increasing sales means more contribution margin above break-even, which translates directly into profit!
ch204.scenario_cut_costs: Smart thinking! Lowering fixed costs reduces the break-even point, making it easier to turn a profit!
ch204.quiz_intro: Let us check your understanding of CVP analysis!
ch204.quiz_breakeven.question: What is the formula for break-even sales?
ch204.quiz_breakeven.correct: Correct! Break-Even Sales = Fixed Costs / Contribution Margin Ratio. This gives you the revenue needed to cover all costs.
ch204.quiz_breakeven.incorrect: Not quite. The correct formula is: Break-Even Sales = Fixed Costs / Contribution Margin Ratio.
ch204.dialog_summary: CVP (Cost-Volume-Profit) analysis helps managers understand the relationship between costs, volume, and profit. The break-even point is where total revenue equals total costs.
ch204.dialog_final: Great job! Next, we will learn about investing in securities and how to account for financial investments!
ch204.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 204 Summary:

- Variable Costs: Change with volume (materials, labor)
- Fixed Costs: Constant regardless of volume (rent, depreciation)
- Contribution Margin = Revenue - Variable Costs
- Break-Even Sales = Fixed Costs / CM Ratio
- CVP analysis guides pricing and volume decisions
ch205.dialog_1: Our lemonade business is doing well! Now let us learn how to invest surplus cash in securities.
ch205.dialog_2: Companies often invest excess cash to earn returns. But different types of securities have different accounting rules.
ch205.dialog_3: The classification depends on the company's intention: short-term trading or holding until maturity.
ch205.dialog_types_intro: There are two main categories we will cover: Trading Securities and Held-to-Maturity Securities.
ch205.dialog_trading_securities: Trading Securities are bought with the intent to sell in the near term for a profit. They are valued at fair (market) value on the balance sheet.
ch205.dialog_htm_securities: Held-to-Maturity Securities are bonds you plan to hold until they mature. They are recorded at acquisition cost (amortized cost).
ch205.dialog_banker_1: Welcome! We have some excellent investment options available today.
ch205.dialog_banker_2: Let us start by purchasing some trading securities worth 5,000G.
ch205.dialog_after_purchase_trading: Trading Securities now appears as an asset on the balance sheet. These will be revalued to market price at each period end.
ch205.show_bs_after_purchase.msg: BS: Trading Securities recorded as a current asset at cost.
ch205.sale_intro: Good news! The market price went up. Let us sell these securities for 5,500G and lock in a gain!
ch205.sale_securities_tx.desc: Sell trading securities at a gain (5,500G)
ch205.dialog_after_sale: We sold for 5,500G what we bought for 5,000G. The 500G difference is a Gain on Sale of Securities, which increases income!
ch205.journal_entry_sale.prompt: Record the sale of trading securities: cost 5,000G, selling price 5,500G.
ch205.journal_entry_sale.correct: Perfect! Cash increases by 5,500, Trading Securities decreases by 5,000, and the 500 gain is recorded as income.
ch205.journal_entry_sale.incorrect: Not quite. Debit Cash 5,500 (received), Credit Trading Securities 5,000 (removed), Credit Gain on Sale 500 (profit).
ch205.journal_entry_sale.hint: Three accounts are involved: Cash goes up by the selling price, Trading Securities goes down by the cost, and the difference is a gain.
ch205.revalue_intro: At the end of the period, any trading securities still held must be revalued to their current market price.
ch205.dialog_revalue_explain: If market value exceeds book value, we record a valuation gain. If market value is lower, we record a valuation loss. This is called mark-to-market accounting.
ch205.dialog_after_revalue: The securities are now carried at 3,200G (cost 3,000 + gain 200). The valuation gain flows through the income statement.
ch205.htm_intro: Now let us purchase Held-to-Maturity bonds. These are recorded at cost and NOT revalued to market price, since we plan to hold them to maturity.
ch205.dialog_after_htm: HTM Securities are valued at amortized cost on the BS. Market fluctuations do not affect their recorded value because we intend to hold them to maturity.
ch205.show_final_bs.msg: BS: Trading Securities at market value, HTM Securities at amortized cost. Different rules for different intentions!
ch205.dialog_summary: Trading securities use fair value (mark-to-market). HTM securities use amortized cost. The key difference is management's intent for holding the investment.
ch205.dialog_final: Well done! Next, we will learn about acquiring subsidiaries and the concept of goodwill!
ch205.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 205 Summary:

- Trading Securities: Short-term, valued at fair/market value
- HTM Securities: Held to maturity, valued at amortized cost
- Gain/Loss on Sale: Selling price - Cost
- Mark-to-Market: Revalue trading securities each period
- Classification depends on management's intent
ch206.dialog_1: Your lemonade empire is expanding! Today we learn about acquiring another company to form a parent-subsidiary relationship.
ch206.dialog_2: When a company buys more than 50% of another company's shares, the buyer becomes the parent and the acquired company becomes a subsidiary.
ch206.dialog_3: But what happens when you pay more for a company than its net assets are worth? That is where goodwill comes in.
ch206.dialog_subsidiary_explain: A subsidiary is a company controlled by the parent. Control means owning more than 50% of voting shares. The parent directs the subsidiary's operations.
ch206.dialog_why_acquire: Companies acquire subsidiaries to expand into new markets, gain technology, or achieve economies of scale. It is a growth strategy.
ch206.choice_acquisition.prompt: How should we approach the acquisition?
ch206.acquire_full: We will acquire 100% of the subsidiary for 8,000G. The subsidiary's net assets (assets minus liabilities) are worth 6,000G.
ch206.dialog_after_acquire: Subsidiary Securities appears as an asset. This represents our ownership stake in the other company.
ch206.show_bs_after_acquire.msg: BS: Subsidiary Securities recorded at acquisition cost of 8,000G.
ch206.goodwill_intro: Now here is the interesting part. We paid 8,000G, but the subsidiary's net assets are only worth 6,000G. What about the extra 2,000G?
ch206.goodwill_recognition_narration: As a consolidation entry, the difference between the subsidiary shares and the subsidiary's net assets is recognized as goodwill.
ch206.dialog_goodwill_explain: That 2,000G premium is called Goodwill. It represents intangible value: brand reputation, customer relationships, skilled workforce, and future earning potential.
ch206.dialog_goodwill_calc: Goodwill = Purchase Price - Fair Value of Net Assets = 8,000 - 6,000 = 2,000G.
ch206.goodwill_narration: Goodwill of 2,000G is recognized on the consolidated balance sheet. It reflects the premium paid above the fair value of identifiable net assets.
ch206.dialog_goodwill_meaning: Goodwill is an intangible asset on the balance sheet. It represents the value of things that cannot be separately identified, like the subsidiary's reputation.
ch206.quiz_intro: Let us check your understanding of goodwill!
ch206.quiz_goodwill.question: What does goodwill represent in an acquisition?
ch206.quiz_goodwill.0: The total price paid for the subsidiary
ch206.quiz_goodwill.1: The excess of purchase price over the fair value of net assets acquired
ch206.quiz_goodwill.2: The fair value of the subsidiary's physical assets
ch206.quiz_goodwill.correct: Correct! Goodwill is the premium paid above the fair value of net assets. It captures intangible value like brand and reputation.
ch206.quiz_goodwill.incorrect: Not quite. Goodwill is specifically the EXCESS of purchase price over the fair value of net assets acquired.
ch206.amortize_intro: Goodwill does not last forever. Under Japanese accounting standards, goodwill is amortized over its estimated useful life (up to 20 years).
ch206.dialog_amortize_explain: Each year, a portion of goodwill is expensed as Goodwill Amortization. This gradually reduces the goodwill asset on the balance sheet.
ch206.dialog_after_amortize: Goodwill decreased by 400G, and Goodwill Amortization expense appeared on the income statement. The intangible value is being systematically expensed.
ch206.show_final_bs.msg: BS: Goodwill reduced by amortization. The intangible asset gradually decreases over time.
ch206.show_final_pl.msg: P&L: Goodwill Amortization of 400G recorded as an expense, reducing net income.
ch206.dialog_summary: When acquiring a subsidiary: record the investment, recognize goodwill for any premium paid, then amortize goodwill over its useful life.
ch206.dialog_final: Great work! Next, we will learn how to prepare consolidated financial statements for the whole group!
ch206.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 206 Summary:

- Subsidiary: Company controlled by parent (>50% ownership)
- Goodwill = Purchase Price - Fair Value of Net Assets
- Goodwill is an intangible asset
- Amortized over useful life (up to 20 years in Japan)
- Amortization is an expense on the P&L
ch207.dialog_1: Now that we own a subsidiary, we need to prepare consolidated financial statements for the entire group.
ch207.dialog_2: Consolidated statements combine the parent and subsidiary as if they were a single economic entity.
ch207.dialog_3: But here is the challenge: transactions between group companies must be eliminated to avoid double-counting.
ch207.dialog_consolidated_intro: Consolidated financial statements show the financial position and performance of the entire corporate group as one unit.
ch207.dialog_consolidated_purpose: The purpose is to give investors and stakeholders a true picture of the group's economic reality, not just the parent's standalone results.
ch207.dialog_consolidated_steps: The consolidation process has three main steps: (1) Combine all accounts, (2) Eliminate intercompany transactions, (3) Recognize non-controlling interests.
ch207.intercompany_intro: When the parent sells goods to a subsidiary (or vice versa), those transactions must be eliminated in consolidation.
ch207.dialog_intercompany_example: For example, our parent company sells lemonade concentrate to the subsidiary for 3,000G. The cost was 2,000G.
ch207.intercompany_sale_narration: The parent records a sale of 3,000G and COGS of 2,000G. But from the group's perspective, this is just moving inventory within the same family.
ch207.dialog_intercompany_problem: If we simply add up parent and subsidiary financials, this intercompany sale inflates both revenue and costs. The group did not actually sell to an outsider!
ch207.dialog_elimination_explain: To fix this, we create an elimination entry that reverses the intercompany transaction in the consolidated statements.
ch207.dialog_elimination_reason: We reverse the parent's revenue of 3,000G and COGS of 2,000G. The remaining 1,000G markup in the subsidiary's inventory is also removed.
ch207.elimination_tx.desc: Eliminate intercompany sale (reverse revenue, COGS, and unrealized profit)
ch207.dialog_after_elimination: After elimination, the consolidated statements show only transactions with outside parties. The group's true economic activity is revealed!
ch207.nci_intro: One more important concept: Non-Controlling Interests (NCI).
ch207.dialog_nci_explain: If we own less than 100% of a subsidiary, the portion we do not own belongs to other shareholders. This is called Non-Controlling Interests.
ch207.dialog_nci_example: For example, if we own 80% of a subsidiary, the remaining 20% is NCI. Their share of the subsidiary's equity and profit is shown separately on consolidated statements.
ch207.show_consolidated_bs.msg: Consolidated BS: Parent + Subsidiary combined, intercompany items eliminated, NCI shown separately.
ch207.quiz_intro: Let us test your understanding of consolidated statements!
ch207.quiz_elimination.question: Why must intercompany transactions be eliminated in consolidated statements?
ch207.quiz_elimination.0: To reduce the group's tax liability
ch207.quiz_elimination.1: To make the subsidiary's accounts disappear
ch207.quiz_elimination.2: To avoid double-counting and show only transactions with outside parties
ch207.quiz_elimination.correct: Correct! Elimination prevents double-counting. Consolidated statements should reflect only the group's transactions with external parties.
ch207.quiz_elimination.incorrect: Not quite. The purpose of elimination is to avoid double-counting and present only the group's transactions with external parties.
ch207.dialog_summary_1: Consolidated statements combine parent and subsidiary financials, eliminate intercompany transactions, and recognize non-controlling interests.
ch207.dialog_summary_2: The goal is to present the economic reality of the entire group as if it were a single entity.
ch207.dialog_final: Excellent! Next, we will learn about dividends, legal reserves, and the statement of changes in equity!
ch207.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 207 Summary:

- Consolidated Statements: Parent + Subsidiary as one entity
- Intercompany Elimination: Remove internal transactions
- Unrealized Profit: Markup on intercompany inventory removed
- Non-Controlling Interests: Outside shareholders' portion
- Goal: Show true group economic reality
ch208.dialog_1: Congratulations! Our group is profitable. Now we need to report to shareholders and decide on profit distribution.
ch208.dialog_2: Shareholders want to know how their investment is doing. The Statement of Changes in Equity shows exactly that.
ch208.dialog_3: It tracks how equity changed during the period: from net income, dividends, and other changes.
ch208.dialog_equity_intro: The Statement of Changes in Equity reconciles the beginning and ending balances of each equity component.
ch208.dialog_equity_components: Key equity components: Share Capital (what investors put in), Retained Earnings (accumulated profits), and Legal Reserve (required savings).
ch208.dialog_retained_earnings: Retained Earnings is the total of all past net income minus all past dividends. It represents profits reinvested in the business.
ch208.dividend_intro: When a company earns a profit, the board may decide to pay dividends to shareholders.
ch208.dialog_dividend_explain: Declaring a dividend reduces Retained Earnings and creates a Dividends Payable liability. Cash does not leave until the actual payment date.
ch208.choice_dividend.prompt: How much should we declare in dividends?
ch208.after_declare: Dividends declared! Retained Earnings decreased and Dividends Payable (liability) increased. The shareholders will receive payment soon.
ch208.journal_entry_dividend_2000.prompt: Record the journal entry for declaring dividends of 2,000G.
ch208.journal_entry_dividend_2000.correc: Perfect! Debit Retained Earnings (equity decreases) and Credit Dividends Payable (liability increases).
ch208.journal_entry_dividend_2000.incorr: Not quite. Debit Retained Earnings (reducing equity) and Credit Dividends Payable (creating a liability to pay shareholders).
ch208.journal_entry_dividend_2000.hint: Declaring dividends reduces equity (Retained Earnings) and creates a liability (Dividends Payable). Which side is debit and which is credit?
ch208.journal_entry_dividend_3000.prompt: Record the journal entry for declaring dividends of 3,000G.
ch208.journal_entry_dividend_3000.correc: Perfect! Debit Retained Earnings (equity decreases) and Credit Dividends Payable (liability increases).
ch208.journal_entry_dividend_3000.incorr: Not quite. Debit Retained Earnings (reducing equity) and Credit Dividends Payable (creating a liability to pay shareholders).
ch208.journal_entry_dividend_3000.hint: Declaring dividends reduces equity (Retained Earnings) and creates a liability (Dividends Payable). Which side is debit and which is credit?
ch208.show_bs_after_declare.msg: BS: Retained Earnings decreased, Dividends Payable (liability) appeared. Equity shifted to liability!
ch208.pay_dividend_intro: Now let us actually pay the dividends to shareholders. This settles the liability.
ch208.pay_dividend_tx_3000.desc: Pay dividends to shareholders (2,000G - partial payment)
ch208.dialog_after_pay: Cash decreased and Dividends Payable was eliminated. The liability is settled. Shareholders received their money!
ch208.legal_reserve_intro: In Japan, companies must set aside a Legal Reserve when paying dividends.
ch208.dialog_legal_reserve_explain: The Legal Reserve protects creditors. Companies must set aside 1/10 of dividends until the reserve reaches 1/4 of share capital. Let us reserve 200G.
ch208.dialog_after_reserve: Retained Earnings decreased by 200G and Legal Reserve increased by 200G. Both are equity, so total equity does not change, but the composition shifts.
ch208.show_final_bs.msg: BS: Retained Earnings reduced by dividends and legal reserve. Legal Reserve and Dividends Payable reflect the distributions.
ch208.dialog_summary_1: Dividends distribute profits to shareholders: Retained Earnings decreases, and cash eventually flows out.
ch208.dialog_summary_2: The Legal Reserve is a mandatory equity reserve in Japan that protects creditors. The Statement of Changes in Equity tracks all these movements.
ch208.dialog_final: Well done! Next, we will learn about the Cash Flow Statement using the indirect method!
ch208.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 208 Summary:

- Statement of Changes in Equity: Tracks equity movements
- Dividends: Retained Earnings(-) / Dividends Payable(+)
- Payment: Dividends Payable(-) / Cash(-)
- Legal Reserve: Mandatory reserve (1/10 of dividends)
- Equity components: Share Capital, Retained Earnings, Legal Reserve
ch209.dialog_1: Today we learn about the third major financial statement: the Cash Flow Statement.
ch209.dialog_2: The balance sheet shows position, the income statement shows performance, and the cash flow statement shows how cash actually moved.
ch209.dialog_3: Remember from earlier chapters: profit and cash are different. The cash flow statement bridges that gap.
ch209.dialog_cf_intro: The cash flow statement explains the change in cash from the beginning to the end of the period. It answers: where did the cash come from and where did it go?
ch209.dialog_three_sections: It is divided into three sections: Operating Activities, Investing Activities, and Financing Activities.
ch209.dialog_operating_cf: Operating Activities show cash from the core business. This is the most important section because it shows whether the business generates cash from its operations.
ch209.dialog_indirect_method: The indirect method starts with net income and adjusts for non-cash items and changes in working capital to arrive at operating cash flow.
ch209.dialog_add_back_depreciation: Depreciation is a non-cash expense. It reduced net income but no cash actually left. So we add it back to calculate operating cash flow.
ch209.dialog_working_capital: Changes in working capital also matter. For example, if accounts receivable increased, that means revenue was earned but cash was not yet collected.
ch209.operating_example_narration: Let us work through an example. We had sales, expenses, and depreciation this period.
ch209.dialog_operating_summary: Net Income = 10,000 - 6,000 - 1,000 = 3,000G. But operating cash flow = 3,000 + 1,000 (add back depreciation) = 4,000G. More cash than profit!
ch209.investing_cf_intro: Investing Activities show cash spent on or received from long-term assets like equipment and securities.
ch209.dialog_investing_explain: Buying equipment is a cash outflow in investing. Selling equipment would be a cash inflow. These are investments for the future.
ch209.dialog_after_investing: The 5,000G equipment purchase is shown as a cash outflow in the investing section. It is not an operating expense, it is a long-term investment.
ch209.financing_cf_intro: Financing Activities show cash from or returned to lenders and shareholders.
ch209.dialog_financing_explain: This includes borrowing, repaying loans, issuing shares, and paying dividends. These transactions involve the company's capital structure.
ch209.loan_borrowing_narration: As a financing example, let us first borrow from the bank.
ch209.loan_borrowing_done: We borrowed 5,000G. Checking Account (asset) increased and Loans Payable (liability) increased. This is a cash inflow from financing activities.
ch209.dialog_after_financing: Loan repayment of 3,000G and dividend payment of 1,000G are both financing cash outflows. Total financing outflow: 4,000G.
ch209.show_cf_report.msg: Cash Flow Statement: Operating +4,000, Investing -5,000, Financing -4,000. Net change: -5,000G.
ch209.dialog_cf_analysis: Even though we had a net cash decrease, the positive operating cash flow is a good sign. The outflows were for investment (growth) and debt reduction (strengthening).
ch209.quiz_intro: Let us test your understanding of the cash flow categories!
ch209.quiz_cf_category.question: Where does depreciation expense appear on the cash flow statement (indirect method)?
ch209.quiz_cf_category.0: Investing activities as a cash outflow
ch209.quiz_cf_category.1: Operating activities as an add-back to net income
ch209.quiz_cf_category.2: Financing activities as a cash outflow
ch209.quiz_cf_category.correct: Correct! Depreciation is added back to net income in the operating section because it is a non-cash expense.
ch209.quiz_cf_category.incorrect: Not quite. Depreciation is a non-cash expense, so it is added back to net income in the operating activities section.
ch209.dialog_summary: The cash flow statement has three sections: Operating (core business cash), Investing (long-term assets), and Financing (capital structure). The indirect method starts from net income.
ch209.dialog_final: Excellent! In the final chapter, we will do a comprehensive review and learn advanced financial ratios!
ch209.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 209 Summary:

- Cash Flow Statement: Explains cash changes over a period
- Three sections: Operating, Investing, Financing
- Indirect Method: Start with Net Income, adjust for non-cash items
- Add back depreciation (non-cash expense)
- Operating CF is the most important indicator
ch210.dialog_1: This is the final chapter! Congratulations on making it this far. Let us bring everything together.
ch210.dialog_2: We will review all three levels, look at the complete financial statements, and learn advanced financial ratios.
ch210.dialog_3: By the end, you will be able to analyze any company's financial health like a professional.
ch210.review_lv1: Level 1 covered the fundamentals: the accounting equation, double-entry bookkeeping, revenue, expenses, and the basic financial statements.
ch210.review_lv2: Level 2 expanded into corporate accounting: incorporation, payroll, taxes, accruals, allowances, fixed assets, and monthly closing.
ch210.review_lv3: Level 3 explored advanced topics: manufacturing costs, standard costing, CVP analysis, securities, subsidiaries, consolidated statements, and cash flows.
ch210.show_review_bs.msg: Balance Sheet: Your complete financial position after all three levels!
ch210.show_review_pl.msg: Income Statement: Revenue, COGS, operating expenses, and net income across the journey!
ch210.show_review_cf.msg: Cash Flow Statement: How cash moved through operating, investing, and financing activities!
ch210.ratio_intro: Now let us learn advanced financial ratios that analysts use to evaluate company performance.
ch210.dialog_roe: Return on Equity (ROE) = Net Income / Shareholders' Equity x 100. It measures how effectively the company uses shareholders' investment to generate profit.
ch210.dialog_equity_ratio: Equity Ratio = Shareholders' Equity / Total Assets x 100. It shows the proportion of assets financed by equity rather than debt. Higher is generally safer.
ch210.dialog_asset_turnover: Asset Turnover = Revenue / Total Assets. It measures how efficiently the company uses its assets to generate sales. Higher means more efficient use of assets.
ch210.dialog_interest_coverage: Interest Coverage Ratio = Operating Income / Interest Expense. It shows how easily the company can pay interest on its debt. Higher is better.
ch210.dialog_dupont: DuPont Analysis breaks ROE into three components: Profit Margin x Asset Turnover x Financial Leverage. This reveals what drives shareholder returns.
ch210.quiz_intro: Time for the final exam! Three questions to test your comprehensive knowledge.
ch210.quiz_1.question: What does ROE (Return on Equity) measure?
ch210.quiz_1.0: How effectively shareholders' equity generates profit
ch210.quiz_1.1: How much cash the company has
ch210.quiz_1.2: The total value of the company's assets
ch210.quiz_1.correct: Correct! ROE = Net Income / Equity. It measures how well the company turns shareholders' investment into profit.
ch210.quiz_1.incorrect: Not quite. ROE measures how effectively shareholders' equity is used to generate profit. The formula is Net Income / Equity.
ch210.quiz_2.question: In the indirect method cash flow statement, why is depreciation added back to net income?
ch210.quiz_2.1: Because it is a financing activity
ch210.quiz_2.2: Because it reduced net income but did not use cash
ch210.quiz_2.correct: Correct! Depreciation is a non-cash expense. It reduced net income on the P&L, but no cash actually left, so we add it back.
ch210.quiz_2.incorrect: Not quite. Depreciation is added back because it is a non-cash expense: it reduced net income but no cash was spent.
ch210.quiz_3.question: What is Goodwill in the context of a business acquisition?
ch210.quiz_3.0: The fair value of the subsidiary's tangible assets
ch210.quiz_3.1: The excess of purchase price over the fair value of net assets acquired
ch210.quiz_3.2: The total liabilities assumed in the acquisition
ch210.quiz_3.correct: Correct! Goodwill = Purchase Price - Fair Value of Net Assets. It represents intangible value like brand, reputation, and synergies.
ch210.quiz_3.incorrect: Not quite. Goodwill is the premium paid above the fair value of net assets. It represents intangible value like brand and reputation.
ch210.dialog_grad_1: You did it! You have completed all 30 chapters across three levels of accounting!
ch210.dialog_grad_2: From a simple lemonade stand to managing a corporate group with subsidiaries, you have come an incredible distance.
ch210.dialog_grad_3: You now understand financial statements, cost accounting, securities, consolidation, and financial analysis.
ch210.dialog_grad_4: Remember, accounting is the language of business. These skills will serve you throughout your career, whether you become an accountant, manager, or entrepreneur.
ch210.dialog_grad_5: Let us take one final look at your complete financial statements. You should be proud of what you have built!
ch210.show_final_bs.msg: Final Balance Sheet: The complete financial position of your lemonade empire!
ch210.show_final_pl.msg: Final Income Statement: All revenues and expenses across your journey!
ch210.show_final_cf.msg: Final Cash Flow Statement: The complete picture of cash movements in your business!
ch210.dialog_final: Congratulations, graduate! You have mastered accounting from basics to advanced analysis. The accounting quest is complete!
ch210.chapter_end.summary:
Chapter 210 - Comprehensive Analysis:

- Review of all three levels (30 chapters)
- ROE: Net Income / Equity
- Equity Ratio: Equity / Total Assets
- Asset Turnover: Revenue / Total Assets
- Interest Coverage: Operating Income / Interest Expense
- DuPont Analysis: Margin x Turnover x Leverage

Game Complete! Congratulations!
